html:
AQA GCSE English Literature — Power and Conflict Anthology — 10 Most Likely Comparison Questions to Come Up (2026)

AQA English Literature Paper 2 — Section B (Anthology Poetry) Q26. 30 marks. Recommended writing time: 45 minutes. AOs: AO1 (response + textual reference) / AO2 (writer's methods) / AO3 (context). AQA prints the FULL TEXT of ONE named anchor poem on the paper; the student then selects ONE OTHER poem from the anthology to compare against it from memory. The stem opens "Compare how poets present [theme] in '[named anchor]' and in one other poem from 'Power and conflict'." — for example, the real 2023 stem was "Compare how poets present the ways people are affected by conflict in 'Kamikaze' and in one other poem from 'Power and conflict'." AQA names the anchor; the second poem is the student's choice.

How this list was generated

This Q-bank was built using Sophicly's prediction model adapted for the Power and Conflict cluster's comparative-question shape (see `Power-and-Conflict-AQA-Prediction-Algorithm.md` in this folder). The algorithm scores each of the 15 anthology poems on six signals — frequency as anchor in past papers, cycle gap since last anchored, coverage gap (poems never anchored in the 2017-2025 window), unexplored-angle signal (unexplored stem-themes for that poem), core-topic boost (the six spine poems AQA returns to most), and an exact-stem-repeat penalty. The cluster has set nine of fifteen poems as anchor across 2017-2025 with no repeats; six poems remain never-set within window and are therefore strong candidates for 2026 even when they are not on the spine. The same priority-order pattern from the per-text Q-banks carries over here: spine poems (Ozymandias, London, My Last Duchess, Charge of the Light Brigade, Exposure, Kamikaze) get core-topic boost despite earlier-window appearances, because AQA cannot avoid the cluster's moral core forever.

Section IDs (Q1–Q10) ARE the the model priority order. This differs from the per-text Q-banks, where the section IDs are stable references and a separate priority table reorders them. Because the P&C anchor-poem ranking is the document's spine, Q1 = highest-confidence anchor, Q10 = lowest within the top 10.

Priority order — rehearse in this order (anchor predictions)

This table ranks the 10 most likely anchor poems (the poem AQA prints in full on the paper) for 2026. The second column lists Sophicly's recommended comparison candidate — the poem these plans rehearse — but in the exam the student is free to choose any other anthology poem that fits the stem's theme.

| the model rank | Predicted anchor poem (AQA names) | Confidence | Likely 2026 stem theme | Sophicly's suggested comparison poem (student choice) |

|---:|---|---|---|---|

| 1 | Charge of the Light Brigade (Tennyson, 1854) | HIGH | Heroism / commemoration in conflict | Bayonet Charge (Hughes) |

| 2 | Ozymandias (Shelley, 1818) | HIGH | Power and time / the futility of human ambition | My Last Duchess (Browning) |

| 3 | My Last Duchess (Browning, 1842) | HIGH | Power and control over others | Ozymandias (Shelley) |

| 4 | Exposure (Owen, 1917-18) | HIGH | Power of nature in conflict | Storm on the Island (Heaney) |

| 5 | Kamikaze (Garland, 2013) | MED-HIGH | Memory across generations | Poppies (Weir) |

| 6 | Poppies (Weir, 2009) | MED | Grief and loss in conflict | Kamikaze (Garland) |

| 7 | War Photographer (Duffy, 1985) | MED | Suffering and indifference | Remains (Armitage) |

| 8 | The Emigrée (Rumens, 1993) | MED | Identity and displacement | Checking Out Me History (Agard) |

| 9 | The Prelude (Wordsworth, 1799-1850) | MED | Power of nature / the individual mind | Storm on the Island (Heaney) |

| 10 | Checking Out Me History (Agard, 2005) | MED | Identity and the power of history | The Emigrée (Rumens) |

Coverage Pack (rehearse but not top-10):

• London (Blake, 1794) — set 2024 at power/place; recency-penalised.

• Tissue (Dharker, 2006) — set 2022 at power; gap rebuilding.

• Remains (Armitage, 2008) — set 2023 at memory/guilt; gap rebuilding.

• Storm on the Island (Heaney, 1966) — set 2019 at power of nature; rotation rules out a return at the same angle.

• Bayonet Charge (Hughes, 1957) — set 2025; recency-penalised.

How to use this document

The AQA question shape. AQA Paper 2 Section B Q26 prints the full text of ONE named anchor poem on the paper. The stem then asks the student to "Compare how poets present [theme] in '[named anchor]' and in one other poem from 'Power and conflict'." The student selects the comparison poem from memory. There is no comparison poem printed — the second poem is the student's free choice from the remaining fourteen.

How this Q-bank works. Each of the ten Qs below predicts a different anchor poem AQA may print in 2026 and rehearses a plan against ONE recommended comparison candidate, plus 2–3 defensible alternatives. Every BP weaves BOTH poems together using AQA's mandated INTEGRATED-COMPARISON shape — BP1 compares FORM, BP2 compares STRUCTURE, BP3 compares LANGUAGE — each BP delivering comparative TTECEA+C so that no sentence is pure-Poem-A or pure-Poem-B. If a student walks into the exam and the printed anchor matches one of the ten predictions but they prefer a different comparison from the alternatives or anywhere else in the anthology, the same plan adapts — the comparison poem swaps in across BP1, BP2 and BP3, keeping the FORM / STRUCTURE / LANGUAGE three-paragraph architecture intact.

Rehearsal priority. Sections Q1–Q10 are already ordered by the model's confidence in the anchor prediction (Q1 = highest, Q10 = lowest within the top 10). If exam time is tight, rehearse the top 5 first (Q1–Q5). For comprehensive coverage, rehearse all 10. Memorise the cross-question reuse table at the end so the same prepared pair-foundations cover multiple Qs.

Substitution rule. In the exam, if a student prefers a comparison poem the plan does not rehearse — say Tissue against Ozymandias instead of My Last Duchess — the plan's anchor-poem content stays valid across BP1, BP2 and BP3; only the comparison-poem side swaps in fresh material. The seven-sentence comparative TTECEA+C shape, the FORM / STRUCTURE / LANGUAGE three-paragraph architecture, and the AO weighting do not change.

Pedagogical depth — three non-negotiables for HIGH-confidence picks

Every Grade-9 essay on a P&C comparison should thread at least one of these moves into one BP or the conclusion. They are the cluster's deepest analytical pivots; Sophia teaches them once so students can deploy them across any Q.

1. Name WHICH power, then ask WHO PAYS — the cluster's deepest counter-reading.

The standard reading of Power and Conflict identifies the anthology as a comparison-set on power-of-humans / power-of-nature / power-of-conflict. The cluster anatomises power of humans over humans (My Last Duchess, London), power of the state (Charge of the Light Brigade, Kamikaze), power of nature (The Prelude, Storm on the Island, Exposure), power of memory (Remains, The Emigrée, Poppies), power of art and language (Ozymandias, Checking Out Me History), and power of time (Ozymandias, Tissue). Every comparison essay should name in its thesis WHICH power the two poems are about and HOW that power operates differently in each.

The sharper counter-reading exposes the cluster's deepest editorial argument: the spine poems are NOT primarily about power — they are about WHAT POWER DESTROYS, and every comparison should ask WHO PAYS. Ozymandias's hubris is destroyed by time (the "colossal Wreck" in the "lone and level sands" names the destruction-of-power as the poem's controlling concept, not power-as-display); the Duchess is destroyed by Duke Ferrara's honour-code (her smile "too easily made glad" names the cost the speaker has extracted); the Light Brigade soldiers are destroyed by command-incompetence (the elegiac refrain "the six hundred" names the count of the dead, not the count of the heroic); the Trenches' soldiers are destroyed by Pity-of-War indifference (Owen's "But nothing happens" names the cost of waiting). The anthology argues that power's signature output is DESTRUCTION, and every comparison should ask WHO PAYS for the power the poem stages. Generic "power" answers receive Band 4 ceiling. Top-band answers specify the power, name the destruction, and identify the victim the power produces. Thread this into: Q1 Charge of the Light Brigade / Q2 Ozymandias / Q3 My Last Duchess / Q4 Exposure / Q5 Kamikaze / Q9 The Prelude / any BP3 that names the comparison's structural argument.

2. The chronological span dramatises power's evolution — Romantic-monarchical-hubris to contemporary state-violence-on-individuals.

The cluster's chronological arc is itself an argument. Romantic poems (Ozymandias, London, The Prelude, Extract from The Prelude) stage power as monarchical or institutional hubris to be critiqued from the outside; the speaker is the Romantic walker, the dispossessed observer, the figure standing outside the inherited authority. Victorian poems (My Last Duchess, Charge of the Light Brigade) stage power as personal control or state command, the form (dramatic monologue, ballad) carrying the moral verdict. War poems (Exposure, Bayonet Charge, Remains) shift the frame from power-as-political-fact to power-as-trauma-on-individuals — the soldier-speaker registers the cost the command-decision produced. Contemporary poems (Kamikaze, Poppies, Tissue, War Photographer) extend the trauma-frame to families, photographers, witnesses, and the materials power leaves behind. The chronological span thus argues that British poetry's relationship to power has moved from external critique (Romantic) through internal dramatisation (Victorian) into traumatised registration (twentieth-century) and finally into ethical witnessing (contemporary). The anthology's pedagogical move is to dramatise this evolution through fifteen poems organised so that the comparison forces the student to register the historical shift. Thread this into: any BP3 that pairs a Romantic poem with a contemporary poem (e.g. Ozymandias / Tissue, London / Checking Out Me History, The Prelude / War Photographer); any conclusion that names the cluster's editorial argument.

3. The comparison must trace the EFFECT DIFFERENTIAL — same theme, different methods, different moral knowledge.

AQA's mark scheme rewards comparison of methods and of effects produced. The strongest essays argue that the two poems share the same theme but produce different effects through different methods — the differential is the analytical event. Charge of the Light Brigade and Bayonet Charge both depict cavalry charges, but Tennyson's ballad commemorates while Hughes's anti-epic strips heroic narrative; the reader leaves Tennyson recruited and Hughes disabused. Naming this differential converts a parallel-description essay into a comparison essay. Without it, the essay catalogues techniques in two poems and gestures at similarity; with it, the essay argues that comparison produces moral knowledge unavailable from either poem alone. The differential is what carries the essay across the Band 5/Band 6 line.

Why this depth matters pedagogically. AQA's Band 6 descriptor asks for "a convincing, critical analysis and exploration" of method, sustained across the comparison. A reading that names WHICH power, asks WHO PAYS, traces the historical shift across the cluster's chronological span, and tracks the EFFECT differential is the species of conceptualisation Examiner Reports name as the top-band signature. Without it, a comparison essay is parallel description; with it, the essay is critical analysis.

A universal critical-angle method. Every text in the AQA spec rewards the same move: identify the standard reading; then identify a counter-reading the same evidence supports; then read the two together as the species of conceptualisation Grade 9 demands. For Power and Conflict the dual readings are power-as-display/power-as-destruction (Ozymandias, MLD, CLB), Romantic-external-critique/contemporary-internal-trauma (the cluster's chronological shift), parallel-description/effect-differential (Q26 structural test). Sophia should generate the second reading whenever helping a student plan — the standard reading is what most students will write; the second reading is what carries them past the band-9 line.

The seven-sentence body paragraph shape — comparative TTECEA+C for AQA integrated comparison

BP1 compares FORM, BP2 compares STRUCTURE, BP3 compares LANGUAGE. Within each BP, every sentence weaves BOTH poems together — no sentence is pure Poem-A or pure Poem-B. The seven sentences deliver comparative TTECEA+C:

1. Comparative topic sentence (AO1) — concept only, "While [Poet A] [concept A], [Poet B] [concept B]" or "Where [Poet A]…, [Poet B]…", framing the conceptual contrast upfront with no techniques named. ~30 words max.

2. Comparative technique + evidence (AO2 + AO1) — name the FORM-related / STRUCTURE-related / LANGUAGE-related technique in each poem AND quote both poems inside the same sentence, then infer what the technique-pair achieves.

3. Comparative close analysis (AO2) — zoom into specific words / sounds / images in each quote, weaving back and forth (Poet A's verb against Poet B's verb; Poet A's sound-pattern against Poet B's sound-pattern).

4. Comparative effects (AO2) — what each poem makes the reader experience, compared. Use precise emotion words (pity, dread, exhaustion, complicity, awe, bewilderment, grief, vertigo, indignation, tenderness, exposure, ironic awe, horrified complicity, claustrophobic dread).

5. Comparative author's purpose (AO1 + AO2) — hedged ("may have written", "can be read as", "a defensible reading is", "perhaps") — why each poet chose that form / structure / language move.

6. Comparative context (AO3) — one period-specific fact for each poet, compared — context driving each poet's specific choice.

7. Synthesising hinge — a single sentence pivoting to the next paragraph (Thus / Whereas / By contrast / However) and summarising what THIS comparative element (form / structure / language) revealed.

Every sentence cap ~40 words. Topic sentence ~30 words max. Every claim anchored to a quote from one or both poems. Concrete connectors only (through / from / leading to / which produces) — no arrows.

Memorise these once

The Power and Conflict anthology argues that power is exercised through violence, language, institutions, and memory — and that conflict, whether between nations or generations, exacts a human cost the powerful rarely see and the powerless cannot escape. Across fifteen poems written between 1794 and 2013, the cluster triangulates this human cost from the Romantic critique of tyranny (Shelley, Blake, Wordsworth) through the Victorian study of personal and state power (Browning, Tennyson) into the war poets' refutation of heroic narrative (Owen, Hughes, Armitage, Weir, Duffy) and finally into the contemporary questions of displacement, identity, and post-colonial reclamation (Dharker, Rumens, Agard, Garland). Two governing tensions run through the cluster: SCALE (who has power and who pays for it) and TIME (whether power-systems eventually crumble or persist within the lifetime of those they damage). Every Grade-9 comparison should hold both.

Key terms (memorise once — useful across multiple Qs):

• Dramatic monologue — verse spoken by a constructed persona whose worldview the form exposes (My Last Duchess; partially Kamikaze, War Photographer).

• Pararhyme — Owen's invention; two end-words share consonant sounds but not vowels ("knive us / nervous"); produces sonic wrongness.

• In medias res — opening mid-action without scene-setting (Bayonet Charge, My Last Duchess).

• Pathetic fallacy — natural phenomena attributed human emotion (Exposure's "melancholy army"; The Prelude's "huge peak, black and huge").

• Praeteritio — saying something by saying you will not say it (the Duke's signature: "how shall I say?").

• Dactylic metre — one stressed, two unstressed syllables ("Half a league, half a league"); produces galloping rhythm.

• Anaphora — repetition at the start of clauses (London's "In every"; Remains's "his bloody life in my bloody hands" repeated).

• Cyclical structure — final image returning to opening (War Photographer's plane-window departure to the next assignment).

• Pity of War — Owen's framework: "the poetry is in the pity"; poetry's task is the truth propaganda denies.

Cluster-specific framing (memorise once — threads into every Power and Conflict BP):

• Romantic critique of authority — Shelley, Blake, Wordsworth wrote during a half-century in which thrones had been toppled and inherited authority delegitimised by the French Revolution. Their poems share a refusal to accept that any inherited structure (monarchy, church, monumental art) is permanent or innocent.

• Pity of War — Owen and the WWI poets refused the inherited heroic register. Tennyson commemorates the Light Brigade in 1854; Owen exposes the trenches in 1918. The same nation, two generations apart, with opposite verdicts on the same activity.

• Honour-code violence — My Last Duchess and Kamikaze, though separated by a century and a hemisphere, both anatomise how an honour code requires violence to maintain itself. Browning's Renaissance Italy and Garland's wartime Japan converge on the same diagnosis.

• Post-colonial reclamation — Agard, Rumens, and Dharker write from the inside of the displacement and erasure colonial systems produced. Their poems share a refusal to accept that identity must be regulated by the institutions that suppressed it.

---

Q1 — Charge of the Light Brigade (predicted anchor) [HIGH confidence — never set as anchor in 9-year window]

Frame

Tennyson's 1854 ballad and Hughes's 1957 anti-epic depict cavalry-charge moments separated by exactly a century, but they argue opposite verdicts about heroism: Tennyson constructs commemoration as civic duty while Hughes systematically dismantles every heroic abstraction patriotic narrative depends on. The reader leaves Tennyson recruited and Hughes disabused. The plan traces three layers of the differential — form (ballad versus anti-epic free verse), structure (commemorative arc versus fragmented freeze-frame), and language (biblical elevation versus stripped abstraction).

Pedagogical depth to thread. The Pity of War shift. Between 1854 and 1918, Britain's collective poetic register on combat reversed. Tennyson writes inside a heroic tradition; Hughes inherits Owen's refusal of that tradition through his father's WWI silence. The comparison stages the moment heroic narrative becomes unsupportable. Land this in BP3 or the conclusion.

Technique gloss:

• Ballad form (Tennyson) — narrative poem designed for oral memorability; ballads are associated with stories the public should not forget.

• Dactylic metre (Tennyson) — one stressed, two unstressed syllables creating a galloping rhythm.

• Refrain (Tennyson) — "the six hundred" recurring across stanzas as a tolling bell.

• In medias res (Hughes) — opening mid-action: "Suddenly he awoke and was running".

• Cosmic imagery (Hughes) — "cold clockwork of the stars and the nations" registering the soldier's existential exposure.

• Anti-epic structure (Hughes) — three-stanza psychological freeze-frame refusing narrative resolution.

• Hook (historical fact tied to thesis): In twenty minutes on the morning of 25 October 1854, over 270 British cavalrymen were killed, wounded, or captured at Balaclava after a miscommunicated order sent them charging Russian artillery — a slaughter Tennyson, as Poet Laureate, was commissioned to reconcile with national honour while Hughes, writing a century later, inherited his father's permanent silence about the Western Front.

• Building sentence (anthology context + comparison axis named): Tennyson writes inside the Victorian heroic tradition that the First World War's trenches would eventually break, while Hughes writes after that break, his anti-epic the inheritance of Owen's Pity of War — that poetry's task is the truth propaganda denies, not the comfort it sells; both poems depict cavalry charges, but their cluster-deep difference is whether heroic narrative remains honestly available to the poet after 1918.

• Thesis: Across form (ballad versus anti-epic free verse), structure (commemorative arc versus fragmented freeze-frame), and language (biblical elevation versus stripped abstraction), Tennyson constructs heroism as civic duty the reader must inherit while Hughes dismantles every heroic abstraction the patriotic register depended on, leaving the reader recruited by the first poem and disabused by the second.

• Comparative topic sentence (AO1): While Tennyson uses the inherited ballad to recruit the reader into civic commemoration, Hughes uses an anti-epic free verse to strip every heroic abstraction patriotic narrative depended on.

• Comparative technique + evidence (AO2 + AO1): Tennyson's dactylic-trochaic ballad meter — "Half a league, half a league, / Half a league onward" — generates a galloping pulse the listener's body must follow, set against Hughes's in-medias-res free verse — "Suddenly he awoke and was running" — which drops the reader inside a soldier's terror without the ballad's communal frame.

• Comparative close analysis (AO2): Tennyson's triple-stressed feet enlist somatic empathy before critical reflection can intervene, while Hughes's adverbial "Suddenly" and the participle "running" refuse any temporal grounding — the ballad's rhythm builds heroic anticipation, the free-verse fragment refuses any anticipation a heroic register could supply.

• Comparative effects (AO2): Thus Tennyson's reader experiences participation in national mourning while Hughes's reader experiences disoriented exposure to a single soldier's terror — the ballad form recruits the listener into the charge, the anti-epic strips the reader of every patriotic vocabulary the inherited form had supplied.

• Comparative author's purpose (AO1 + AO3): Tennyson may have chosen the ballad because ballads are designed to be remembered and recited by communities — the form converting disaster into civic inheritance — whereas Hughes can be read as choosing the anti-epic because the inherited heroic form had become unsupportable after 1918, the formal refusal itself the moral position.

• Comparative context (AO3): Tennyson wrote in 1854 as Poet Laureate, responding to William Howard Russell's Times despatch from Balaclava under public pressure to reconcile catastrophic military failure with national honour, while Hughes wrote in 1957 inside the Pity-of-War tradition Owen had bequeathed, his father's permanent silence about the Western Front the personal source of his refusal.

• Synthesising hinge: Thus form itself encodes the moral verdict each poet renders — Tennyson's ballad converts slaughter into commemoration the listener can inherit, while Hughes's anti-epic registers slaughter as terror no inherited register can metabolise.

• Comparative topic sentence (AO1): Where Tennyson structures the poem as a narrative arc completing in commemoration, Hughes structures it as a psychological freeze-frame refusing the resolution the heroic narrative depended on.

• Comparative technique + evidence (AO2 + AO1): Tennyson's six-stanza chronological progression closes with the imperative refrain "Honour the charge they made! / Honour the Light Brigade" converting narrative into civic instruction, while Hughes's three-stanza interrupted progression closes with "a running man who could not stop" converting narrative into permanent suspension.

• Comparative close analysis (AO2): Tennyson's imperative "Honour" repeats across the closing stanza to convert reading into participation, while Hughes's modal "could not" leaves the soldier stuck inside a present-tense terror the chronological close-down would have erased — one form completes, the other refuses to complete.

• Comparative effects (AO2): Therefore Tennyson's reader experiences narrative resolution through ritual closure, while Hughes's reader experiences narrative collapse — the recurring refrain "the six hundred" tolls like a bell across Tennyson's stanzas, whereas the existential question "In what cold clockwork of the stars and the nations / Was he the hand pointing that second?" halts Hughes's narrative mid-action with no answer the form will supply.

• Comparative author's purpose (AO1 + AO3): Tennyson may have structured the poem to make catastrophic failure bearable by converting it into recurrent ritual the public could repeat, while Hughes can be read as structuring the poem to make patriotic abstraction visible at the moment combat dissolves it — the dropped abstractions "King, honour, human dignity, etcetera" falling away precisely because the structure refuses to recover them.

• Comparative context (AO3): Tennyson wrote inside Victorian honour culture for a public newspaper-reading audience that demanded civic resolution, while Hughes wrote inside the post-WWI generation that had inherited Owen's "poetry is in the pity" — the same cavalry-charge subject mediated by opposite structural responsibilities.

• Synthesising hinge: Thus structure determines what the form can complete — Tennyson's chronological arc completes commemoration the listener can carry away, while Hughes's freeze-frame refuses any completion the heroic narrative could survive.

• Comparative topic sentence (AO1): While Tennyson's biblical-elevated diction transforms slaughter into apocalyptic spectacle, Hughes's stripped colloquial register reduces combat to animal instinct the patriotic vocabulary cannot accommodate.

• Comparative technique + evidence (AO2 + AO1): Tennyson deploys biblical allusion — "Into the jaws of Death, / Into the mouth of hell / Rode the six hundred" — converting cavalry into damned souls, set against Hughes's animalistic simile — "a yellow hare that rolled like a flame / And crawled in a threshing circle" — converting men into terrified prey.

• Comparative close analysis (AO2): Tennyson's metaphors "jaws" and "mouth" personify Death and Hell as devouring agents that elevate the soldiers' fate into mythic register, while Hughes's adjective "yellow" (the colour of cowardice and fear) and the verb "crawled" (animal-low, undignified) strip away every dignifying abstraction — biblical register sanctifies, animal register exposes.

• Comparative effects (AO2): Therefore Tennyson's reader experiences awe at heroic damnation while Hughes's reader experiences pity at human terror — the elevated diction recruits the reader into national mythology, while the stripped diction produces the reader's recognition that combat reduces patriotic vocabulary itself to "luxuries" the soldier discards mid-charge.

• Comparative author's purpose (AO1 + AO3): Tennyson may have deployed biblical register to convert military disaster into spiritual narrative the Victorian public could absorb, while Hughes can be read as deploying animalistic diction to refuse every elevation patriotic narrative required — the Latinate dismissal "etcetera" itself an act of linguistic refusal Tennyson's form would never permit.

• Comparative context (AO3): Tennyson wrote inside a Victorian Britain in which biblical register remained the public's primary vocabulary for civic gravity, while Hughes wrote inside a post-1918 Britain that had heard Owen's "My friend, you would not tell with such high zest / To children ardent for some desperate glory / The old Lie" — the same charge mediated by opposite verbal repertoires.

• Synthesising hinge: Thus language itself encodes the heroic verdict — Tennyson's biblical-elevated vocabulary recruits the reader into commemoration, while Hughes's stripped animal-and-mechanical register strips every abstraction the patriotic vocabulary had been constructed to sustain.

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Tennyson and Hughes both write the cavalry charge, but the century separating them — and Owen's intervening refusal of heroic register — produces opposite verdicts: Tennyson constructs heroism as civic duty the reader inherits, while Hughes dismantles every patriotic abstraction the inherited register depended on.

• Controlling concept: Heroism in the cluster is not a stable category but a vocabulary whose availability to the poet depends on what the poet's nation can afford to remember — Victorian Britain could afford Tennyson's elevation; post-1918 Britain could not.

• Authors' purposes contrasted: Tennyson may have written to reconcile catastrophic military failure with national honour for a public demanding an account, while Hughes can be read as writing to make the silence his father lived inside finally speakable for a son who inherited the war as compressed fragments.

• Ultimate moral about what comparing them reveals: Comparing the two reveals that the truthful representation of combat shifts with the historical moment of the witness — and that poetry's deepest responsibility is not to commemorate the dead but to refuse the comfort that would let the reader forget how they died.

Q2 — Ozymandias (predicted anchor) [HIGH confidence — spine poem + 9-year gap from 2017]

Frame

Shelley's 1818 sonnet and Browning's 1842 dramatic monologue both anatomise absolute power, but their verdicts diverge along the cluster's deepest axis: Shelley's tyrant is comprehensively dismantled by time while Browning's tyrant still holds the curtain string. The plan traces the time-axis differential — Romantic faith that monumental power crumbles versus Victorian recognition that aristocratic untouchability persists within the lifetime of those it damages.

Pedagogical depth to thread. Name WHICH power. Shelley's poem is about the power of monumental ambition over time; Browning's is about the power of dynastic name over personal life. Both are absolute powers, but they operate on different scales and produce different outcomes. The comparison stages the cluster's central tension between scale and time.

Technique gloss:

• Broken sonnet (Shelley) — fourteen-line form deliberately departing from both Petrarchan and Shakespearean schemes; the fracture is the argument.

• Triple-framed narrative (Shelley) — poet receives from traveller, traveller from desert, desert from sculptor.

• Dramatic monologue (Browning) — verse spoken by a constructed persona whose worldview the form exposes.

• Praeteritio (Browning) — saying something by saying you will not say it ("how shall I say?").

• Heroic couplets (Browning) — rhymed iambic pentameter performing aristocratic refinement.

• Caesura (Browning) — line-break enacting the act of refusal ("I choose / Never to stoop").

• Hook (historical fact tied to thesis): In the winter of 1817-18, a seven-ton granite torso of Ramesses II was being unloaded at the British Museum after a sixteen-month haul from Thebes — a fragment of the pharaoh that Shelley, in a sonnet contest with Horace Smith, never saw but used to demolish the very idea of monumental permanence the museum was constructed to celebrate.

• Building sentence (anthology context + comparison axis named): Browning, writing twenty-four years later in a Victorian Britain whose Industrial Revolution was already legislating chartered streets while leaving aristocratic untouchability intact, would draw on the historical Alfonso II of Ferrara to construct a tyrant who — unlike Shelley's pharaoh — was not dismantled by time but rather preserved his authority across centuries through the dramatic monologue's deliberate disclosure to a marriage-broker's envoy.

• Thesis: Across form (broken sonnet versus dramatic monologue), structure (triple-framed dismantling versus single sustained address), and language (Romantic critique of imperative versus Victorian study of praeteritio), Shelley argues that monumental power is comprehensively erased by time while Browning argues that aristocratic untouchability persists within the lifetime of those it damages — producing in the reader awe at one tyrant's collapse and dread at the other's continued reign.

• Comparative topic sentence (AO1): While Shelley uses a broken sonnet to enact monumental power's collapse, Browning uses a dramatic monologue to preserve aristocratic power across the centuries Shelley's pharaoh did not survive.

• Comparative technique + evidence (AO2 + AO1): Shelley's broken sonnet — neither Petrarchan nor Shakespearean, half-rhymed "stone / frown" — opens with "Two vast and trunkless legs of stone / Stand in the desert", set against Browning's dramatic monologue with iambic-pentameter heroic couplets opening "That's my last Duchess painted on the wall, / Looking as if she were alive".

• Comparative close analysis (AO2): Shelley's half-rhyme "stone / frown" refuses the sonnet tradition's ear-satisfying closure to mirror the pharaoh's broken legacy, while Browning's perfect rhyme "wall / call" seals every couplet shut to mirror the Duke's seamless self-presentation — the broken form refuses to immortalise, the polished form refuses to crack.

• Comparative effects (AO2): Thus Shelley's reader experiences ironic awe at the gap between intended grandeur and the form's fractured execution, while Browning's reader experiences horrified complicity at the Duke's polished delivery — the sonnet that should have praised becomes the poem that dismantles, the monologue that should have exposed becomes the speech that controls.

• Comparative author's purpose (AO1 + AO3): Shelley may have chosen the sonnet because it was the literary form most associated with permanence, deliberately shattering it to argue that no structure built to immortalise power can outlast time — whereas Browning can be read as choosing the dramatic monologue because the form's built-in mechanism of deliberate disclosure lets the Duke explain his terms to the envoy without ever stooping to confession.

• Comparative context (AO3): Shelley wrote in 1818 as a Romantic radical at the end of a revolutionary half-century in which thrones had been toppled and Egyptian antiquities reduced to museum curiosities, while Browning wrote in 1842 drawing on Alfonso II of Ferrara — a Renaissance honour-culture in which a wife's smile constituted offence enough for execution — for Victorian readers increasingly sceptical of inherited authority but unable to dislodge it.

• Synthesising hinge: Thus the choice of form is the poet's first verdict on the tyrant's fate — Shelley's broken sonnet enacts dismantling that time delivers, while Browning's dramatic monologue enacts preservation the form's polish guarantees.

• Comparative topic sentence (AO1): Where Shelley's triple-framed narrative removes the pharaoh from any present in which his commands could still operate, Browning's single sustained monologue preserves the Duke's voice unbroken across every line of the poem.

• Building sentence (anthology context + comparison axis named): Heaney, writing 167 years later in 1966 from pre-Troubles Northern Ireland with the political weather already audible, would use a free-verse meditation on an Atlantic storm to refuse the redemptive arc Wordsworth had constructed; both poets stage human confidence collapsing under nature, but Wordsworth's encounter educates while Heaney's only terrifies.

• Thesis: Across form (blank-verse epic versus single-stanza free verse), structure (three-phase Romantic sublime arc versus two-phase movement halting at the storm's peak), and language (personification giving nature moral consciousness versus military metaphor importing political menace), Wordsworth presents nature's power as a moral teacher whose terror produces philosophical growth while Heaney presents it as a force that refuses to identify itself or resolve, the comparison staging the cluster's deepest question about whether overwhelming experience can be made meaningful.

• Comparative topic sentence (AO1): While Wordsworth uses blank-verse epic to stage the Romantic-sublime arc that metabolises terror into philosophical growth, Heaney uses single-stanza free verse to refuse the resolution the inherited Romantic form supplied.

• Comparative technique + evidence (AO2 + AO1): Wordsworth's unrhymed iambic pentameter — "One summer evening (led by her) I found / A little boat tied to a willow tree / Within a rocky cove, its usual home" — opens the poem inside the form Milton had bequeathed for psychological epic, set against Heaney's nineteen-line single-stanza free verse — "We are prepared: we build our houses squat... wizened earth has never troubled us / With hay" — refusing the inherited form from its first line.

• Comparative close analysis (AO2): Wordsworth's blank verse reserves the philosophical space the boy needs to metabolise the encounter, the form modelling the Romantic-sublime arc itself, while Heaney's free verse — flecked with broken iambic pulses — refuses the consoling order the inherited form had constructed, the absent rhyme registering the absent shelter.

• Comparative effects (AO2): Thus Wordsworth's reader experiences awe in the Romantic sublime register where terror ultimately serves understanding, while Heaney's reader is trapped inside a single unresolved encounter the form refuses to release — completion as opposed to formal refusal.

• Comparative author's purpose (AO1 + AO3): Wordsworth may have chosen blank verse to elevate a boyish boat-theft into a moral parable, the Miltonic register inviting the reader to receive nature as moral teacher, whereas Heaney can be read as choosing free verse because the inherited Romantic form had been disqualified by the political weather his Northern Ireland community already heard approaching.

• Comparative context (AO3): Wordsworth drafted the boat-stealing episode in the 1790s beside Ullswater inside Romantic pantheism that attributed conscious moral agency to the natural world, while Heaney published in 1966 inside pre-Troubles Northern Ireland whose sectarian tensions erupted into civil-rights marches two years later — opposite political moments.

• Synthesising hinge: Thus the choice of form is the poet's first verdict on nature's instructive capacity — Wordsworth's blank verse reserves space for resolution, Heaney's free verse refuses the form that resolution would require.

• Comparative topic sentence (AO1): Where Wordsworth structures the poem as a three-phase Romantic sublime arc — confident theft, terrifying encounter, post-experience reflection — Heaney structures it as a two-phase movement that halts at the storm's peak without supplying the third phase.

• Comparative technique + evidence (AO2 + AO1): Wordsworth's three-phase progression moves from "It was an act of stealth / And troubled pleasure" through "a huge peak, black and huge, / As if with voluntary power instinct, / Upreared its head" to "huge and mighty forms, that do not live / Like living men, moved slowly through the mind / By day, and were a trouble to my dreams", set against Heaney's two-phase movement from "We are prepared" to "strange, it is a huge nothing that we fear" halting at the storm's peak.

• Comparative close analysis (AO2): Wordsworth's structural close converts terror into reflective understanding — the "darkness" and "unknown modes of being" metabolising the mountain's pursuit into moral education — while Heaney's structural arrest leaves the islanders inside the encounter, the absent third phase itself the formal refusal of the consoling resolution.

• Comparative effects (AO2): Therefore Wordsworth's reader experiences fear that ultimately serves understanding while Heaney's reader is trapped inside an unresolved encounter — the sublime arc produces quietened reflection, the halted structure produces existential dread.

• Comparative author's purpose (AO1 + AO3): Wordsworth may have structured the poem so that the boy's interior landscape is permanently reshaped by the encounter, the form arguing that nature instructs through terror, whereas Heaney can be read as refusing the structural completion because the political weather of 1966 had already disqualified the resolution the Romantic form would have supplied.

• Comparative context (AO3): Wordsworth wrote inside the Lake District whose mountains genuinely dwarfed the human and inside Romantic pantheism's confidence that nature was spiritual teacher, while Heaney wrote inside a pre-Troubles Northern Ireland whose hostile "wizened earth" lacked trees because the landscape was as politically charged as the title's anagram STORMONT — opposite structural responsibilities.

• Synthesising hinge: Thus structure encodes whether the encounter can be made meaningful — Wordsworth's three-phase arc completes the resolution, Heaney's two-phase halt refuses the completion the inherited form had supplied.

• Comparative topic sentence (AO1): While Wordsworth's personification gives nature moral consciousness that pursues with corrective purpose, Heaney's oxymoron empties nature of identifiable substance the islanders could prepare against.

• Comparative technique + evidence (AO2 + AO1): Wordsworth deploys personification with anthropomorphic verbs — "a huge peak, black and huge, / As if with voluntary power instinct, / Upreared its head... Strode after me" — set against Heaney's military metaphor inside oxymoron — "We are bombarded by the empty air... it is a huge nothing that we fear" — the same adjective "huge" deployed for opposite verdicts.

• Comparative close analysis (AO2): Wordsworth's anthropomorphic verbs "Upreared" and "Strode" register the mountain as pursuing parental authority that has converted geological fact into moral education, while Heaney's military verbs "strafes," "bombarded," "salvo" convert natural force into political assault before the oxymoron "huge nothing" empties the force of substance.

• Comparative effects (AO2): Therefore Wordsworth's reader experiences nature as full hostile agent that ultimately teaches while Heaney's reader experiences nature as terror without identifiable agent — fullness produces understanding, emptied military metaphor produces existential dread.

• Comparative author's purpose (AO1 + AO3): Wordsworth may have deployed personification to argue that the natural world possesses conscious moral agency, the Romantic pantheist conviction itself, whereas Heaney can be read as deploying the emptied military metaphor because the political weather of 1966 refused identification as a force the islanders could prepare against — language itself registering the inherited Romantic vocabulary's disqualification.

• Comparative context (AO3): Wordsworth wrote inside Romantic pantheism's confidence that the natural world was teacher, while Heaney wrote inside a Northern Ireland whose landscape was as politically charged as the political weather audible in the title's anagram — opposite vocabularies for opposite verdicts.

• Synthesising hinge: Thus language itself encodes whether nature can be made meaningful — Wordsworth's personification gives nature moral consciousness that teaches, Heaney's emptied military oxymoron refuses every identifiable agent the inherited Romantic vocabulary had supplied.

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Wordsworth and Heaney both stage human confidence collapsing under nature, but the resolution along which their encounters stand produces opposite verdicts: Wordsworth's terror metabolises into philosophical growth while Heaney's terror refuses to resolve.

• Controlling concept: Nature's power in the cluster is mediated by the historical moment of the witness — Wordsworth's Lake District pantheism turns nature into moral teacher; Heaney's pre-Troubles Northern Ireland turns nature into political weather that refuses identification.

• Authors' purposes contrasted: Wordsworth may have written the Romantic sublime as moral education, while Heaney can be read as refusing that education because the political weather of 1966 had already made it unsupportable — both poets registering nature's overwhelming power, but reaching opposite verdicts on whether the encounter teaches.

• Ultimate moral about what comparing them reveals: Comparing the two reveals that the cluster's deepest move on nature is the refusal of the Romantic frame — that overwhelming experience in the modern period cannot always be metabolised into philosophical growth, and that the form's responsibility is to register the refusal rather than supply the consolation.

Q10 — Checking Out Me History (predicted anchor) [MED confidence — never set as anchor in 9-year window]

Frame

Agard's 2005 Caribbean-creole counter-narrative and Rumens's 1993 free-verse meditation both stage history as institutional power over identity, but their responses diverge: Agard reclaims through public counter-naming while Rumens preserves through private memory. The plan traces the reclamation-versus-preservation differential. This Q reverses Q8's anchor — same poems, different anchor focus.

Pedagogical depth to thread. History in the cluster operates as power exercised through what gets included and excluded from the official record. Both poems refuse institutional authority over the record, but their methods differ.

Technique gloss: As Q8 with anchor reversed; FORM / STRUCTURE / LANGUAGE BPs each still weave both poems.

• Hook (historical fact tied to thesis): Mary Seacole — the Jamaican-born nurse who funded her own passage to the Crimea in 1854 and treated British soldiers at her "British Hotel" near Balaclava — was systematically excluded from the British school curriculum until the late twentieth century, an erasure Agard's 2005 poem corrects by elevating her among the suppressed figures the colonial curriculum had taught generations not to know.

• Building sentence (anthology context + comparison axis named): Rumens, writing twelve years earlier in 1993 from the Cold-War era in which millions of homelands had become inaccessible memories overnight, used a Mediterranean-suggestive unnamed city to register a different response to institutional erasure — the exile's private archive of "sunlight-clear" memory; both poets refuse institutional authority over the record, but their methods occupy opposite ends of the public-private axis.

• Thesis: Across form (Caribbean-creole alternating typography versus free verse with fairy-tale opening), structure (alternating dialectic of colonial fact and Caribbean counter-fact versus three-stanza escalation through threat to defiance), and language (active present-continuous and sculptural metaphor versus passive verbs of marking and the verb "smuggle"), Agard reclaims history through public counter-naming while Rumens preserves it through private memory, both poets together refusing the institutional authority that determines what history a person is permitted to inherit.

• Comparative topic sentence (AO1): While Agard uses Caribbean-creole alternating typography to reclaim history through public counter-naming, Rumens uses Standard-English free verse with a fairy-tale opening to preserve history through the exile's private archive.

• Comparative technique + evidence (AO2 + AO1): Agard's Caribbean-creole free verse with alternating italic-and-roman typography opens on anaphoric repetition — "Dem tell me / Dem tell me / Wha dem want to tell me" — set against Rumens's three eight-line stanzas opening with fairy-tale convention — "There once was a country... I left it as a child / but my memory of it is sunlight-clear".

• Comparative close analysis (AO2): Agard's creole "Dem" refuses the Standard-English literary tradition as an inheritance of colonial authority, while the alternating typography stages the dialectic at the visual level, set against Rumens's fairy-tale opening softening the politics under which the violent verbs of marking operate — refused register as opposed to assimilated register.

• Comparative effects (AO2): Thus Agard's reader witnesses liberation in real time as the typographic shifts enact the "checking out" the title names, while Rumens's reader experiences courageous intimacy accompanying the speaker into hostile territory — public reclamation as opposed to private preservation.

• Comparative author's purpose (AO1 + AO3): Agard can be read as choosing Caribbean creole because every community's words attach people to place — the creole itself part of the identity being reclaimed — whereas Rumens may have chosen the unnamed city and fairy-tale opening to let the poem stand for any exile anywhere.

• Comparative context (AO3): Agard wrote in 2005 from post-colonial Caribbean diaspora, born in Guyana in 1949 and moved to Britain in 1977 inside Macaulay's 1835 Minute on Education legacy of curriculum-erasure, while Rumens wrote in 1993 from the Cold-War era when millions of homelands had become inaccessible memories overnight.

• Synthesising hinge: Thus the choice of form encodes whether response is public or private — Agard's creole alternating typography reclaims history outwardly, Rumens's Standard-English free verse preserves it inwardly.

• Comparative topic sentence (AO1): Where Agard structures the poem as an alternating dialectic of colonial-fact and Caribbean-counter-fact staged through typographic shifts, Rumens structures it as a three-stanza escalation from idealised memory through threat to defiant assertion.

• Comparative technique + evidence (AO2 + AO1): Agard's alternating refrain — "Dem tell me bout 1066 and all dat... But Toussaint L'Ouverture / no dem never tell me bout dat" — culminates in the structural pivot "But now I checking out me own history / I carving out me identity", set against Rumens's escalation from "my memory of it is sunlight-clear" through "I have no passport, there's no way back" to "They mutter death, / and my shadow falls as evidence of sunlight".

• Comparative close analysis (AO2): Agard's monosyllabic "But" performs the entire move from passive reception to active reclamation in a single syllable, converting the speaker from object of colonial narrative to maker of his own image, while Rumens's pivot intensifies the sunlight metaphor as persecution mounts — "sunlight-clear" through "branded by an impression of sunlight" to "my shadow falls as evidence of sunlight".

• Comparative effects (AO2): Therefore Agard's reader witnesses liberation in real time as the typographic shifts enact the dialectic visibly, while Rumens's reader accompanies the exile into hostile territory finding the emotional landscape grows brighter under pressure — visible dialectic as opposed to internal escalation.

• Comparative author's purpose (AO1 + AO3): Agard can be read as structuring the dialectic to make the colonial curriculum's selective inclusion visible at the textual level — the typography itself the political act — whereas Rumens may have structured the escalation so the homeland's light intensifies as persecution mounts, preservation made defiant.

• Comparative context (AO3): Agard wrote inside the legacy of the colonial curriculum that taught generations to know English history while erasing Caribbean and African contributions, while Rumens wrote inside the Cold-War-era political exile that had transformed millions of homelands into memories overnight — opposite structural responsibilities.

• Synthesising hinge: Thus structure determines whether history is reclaimed outwardly or preserved inwardly — Agard's alternating dialectic makes public reclamation textual, Rumens's escalation makes private memory defiant.

• Comparative topic sentence (AO1): While Agard's present-continuous-and-sculptural-metaphor diction registers history as ongoing labour the speaker actively constructs, Rumens's passive-verb-and-compound-adjective diction registers history as permanent imprint the exile cannot lose.

• Comparative technique + evidence (AO2 + AO1): Agard deploys present-continuous and sculptural metaphor — "But now I checking out me own history / I carving out me identity" — and the accusatory pronoun "Dem" — "Dem tell me bout Florence Nightingale and she lamp... but dem never tell me bout Mary Seacole" — set against Rumens's passive verbs of marking inside compound adjectives — "now I am branded by an impression of sunlight" and the closing "smuggle it across the border".

• Comparative close analysis (AO2): Agard's "carving" registers history-construction as ongoing labour and "Dem" registers the colonial education as deliberate narrative-control mechanism, set against Rumens's passive "branded" registering the homeland's mark as cattle-mark or criminal-mark while "sunlight-clear" makes that mark luminous rather than disfiguring.

• Comparative effects (AO2): Therefore Agard's reader experiences liberation in real time as the present-continuous performs the reclamation it describes, while Rumens's reader experiences courageous intimacy as the metaphor intensifies under pressure — active construction as opposed to passive imprint.

• Comparative author's purpose (AO1 + AO3): Agard can be read as deploying the sculptural present-continuous to insist that history is made not given — the figure recovering authorship the curriculum had denied — whereas Rumens may have deployed the passive verbs to register history as imprint exile cannot erase even as the political forces make identity contraband.

• Comparative context (AO3): Agard wrote inside post-colonial Caribbean diaspora where the suppressed figures — Toussaint L'Ouverture, Nanny de Maroon, Mary Seacole — were heroes the British curriculum had systematically excluded, while Rumens wrote inside the Cold-War political exile of the early 1990s when persistent present tense was the only tense exiles could deploy without confirming the homeland was lost.

• Synthesising hinge: Thus language itself encodes whether history is claimed publicly or held privately — Agard's present-continuous and sculptural verbs construct history at the public level the curriculum had refused, Rumens's passive imprint and contraband-verbs preserve history inside the exile's body.

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Agard and Rumens both stage history as institutional power over identity, but their responses occupy opposite ends of the public-private axis — Agard reclaiming through public counter-naming while Rumens preserves through private memory.

• Controlling concept: History in the cluster's contemporary poems operates as power exercised through what gets included and excluded from the official record — and the question of whether response is reclamation or preservation is the deepest analytical move the cluster's post-colonial poems offer.

• Authors' purposes contrasted: Agard can be read as writing the post-colonial counter-narrative that the curriculum had suppressed, while Rumens may have written the exile's archive that political pressure had made contraband — both poets refusing institutional authority over the record.

• Ultimate moral about what comparing them reveals: Comparing the two reveals that the cluster's deepest move on history is the recognition that what counts as history is itself a form of power — and that whether the response is public reclamation or private preservation, the labour of identity is the labour of refusing the institutions that determined what history a person was permitted to inherit.

Two critical voices to deploy

Sophicly deploys two named critical voices across the P&C corpus. Use them sparingly — once per essay maximum — and only when they sharpen the close analysis.

• William Dowling (Rutgers) on My Last Duchess — attribute as "Rutgers critic" or "Rutgers lecturer". The reading: the Duke is a "monster of pride", "untouchable because he's got wealth, power and ancestry"; "I choose / Never to stoop" are "the most menacing words in English poetry" because they encode willingness to kill rather than yield. Use in Q2, Q3 (Browning side).

• Helen Vendler on form-as-meaning. The reading: form is the first argument a poem makes; when the form fits the content, the poem reinforces its argument; when the form fights the content (Shelley's broken sonnet about a broken tyrant; Heaney's free verse about a community that has no formal shelter), the contradiction IS the argument. Use across any Q where formal differential is the analytical event — particularly Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4, Q9.

(Attribute readings to these named voices; do not name Mr Bruff in essays. Bruff is a teaching guide for revision, not an academic source for citation.)

Final reminder bullet

• Name WHICH power. Power and Conflict is multivalent; every comparison thesis must specify which power the two poems anatomise.

• Trace the EFFECT differential. Comparison reveals what parallel description cannot: the methods are the same theme, the effects on the reader are the analytical event.

• One anchor quote per BP — single line, embodying the topic-sentence concept.

• Discourse markers when switching poets — Thus / Whereas / By contrast / However / Indeed / In conclusion.

• Hedged author's-purpose claims — may have / can be read as / a defensible reading is.

• No "shows" as analytical verb — illustrates / portrays / registers / enacts / dramatises / exposes / reveals.

• No arrows — through / from / leading to / which produces.

• No feminist-critique framework — reframe gender as honour-code / dynastic marriage / property-law / social order.

• Hook must tie thesis — fact / quote / question / metaphor that encapsulates THIS essay's specific argument.

• Sentence cap ~35-45 words / ~3 lines. Split rather than truncate.
